does: This paper proposes Vision-guided Direct Preference Optimization (V-DPO), a training method that reduces hallucination in large vision-language models by mitigating the language model backbone's over-reliance on textual priors at the expense of visual grounding. The method applies preference learning to enhance visual context understanding during training. The authors construct a synthetic dataset containing both response-contrast and image-contrast preference pairs to train and evaluate V-DPO, comparing it against human-annotated hallucination samples. The approach demonstrates significant improvements across multiple hallucination benchmarks, with particular strength in learning from image-contrast preference data where visual input directly guides model behavior away from spurious text-based predictions.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to address hallucination in vision-language models, a well-documented problem where models generate plausible but visually inconsistent descriptions. It extends direct preference optimization (DPO), a popular alignment technique from language model training, to the multimodal setting by incorporating vision-specific guidance. The paper responds to prior analysis showing that LVLMs inherit problematic language model biases and insufficient visual grounding from their LLM backbones. It connects to broader research on preference learning for alignment and builds on earlier work investigating the tension between language priors and visual fidelity in multimodal generation, offering a training-time intervention rather than post-hoc decoding modifications.
enables: V-DPO opens pathways for more systematically addressing hallucination through preference-based training in vision-language systems. The synthetic preference data construction methodology could generalize to other multimodal alignment problems beyond hallucination, such as improving reasoning or consistency across modalities. This work enables future research into how different preference pair types (image-contrast versus response-contrast) interact with model architectures and training dynamics. The approach potentially scales to other multimodal models combining vision with language for generation or understanding. It suggests that preference learning frameworks designed for language models can be effectively adapted for vision-language tasks, opening broader applications in aligning multimodal systems with human expectations and visual reality.
